turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Very well. Is there any other cross-examination? Then, does Dr. Kranzbuehler wish to re-examine? Dr. Kranzbuehler, I see it is nearly half-past eleven, so perhaps we had better adjourn for ten minutes. [A recess was taken.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Before Dr. Kranzbuehler goes on with his re-examination, I shall announce the Tribunal's decisions with reference to the applications which were made recently in court. The first application on behalf of the Defendant Von Schirach was for a witness Hans Marsalek to be produced for cross-examination, and that application is granted. The second application was for interrogatories to a witness Kaufmann, and that is granted. The next matter was an application on behalf of the Defendant Hess for five documents; and as to that, the Tribunal orders that two of the documents applied for under Heads B and D in Dr. Seidl's application have already been published in the Reichsgesetzblatt, and one of them is already in evidence, and they Will, therefore, be admitted. The Tribunal considers that the documents applied for under Heads C and E of Dr. Seidl's application are unsatisfactory and have no evidential value; and since it does not appear from Dr. Seidl's application and the matters referred to therein that the alleged copies are copies of any original documents, the application is denied in respect thereof. But leave is granted to Dr. Seidl to file a further affidavit by Gaus covering his recollection of what was in the alleged agreements. The application on behalf of the Defendant Funk for an affidavit by a witness called Kallus is granted. The application on behalf of the Defendant Streicher is denied. The application on behalf of the Defendant Sauckel firstly for a witness named Biedermann is granted, and secondly for four documents; that application is also granted. The application on behalf of the Defendant Seyss-Inquart for an interrogatory to Dr. Stuckart is granted. The application on behalf of the' Defendant Frick is granted for an interrogatory to a witness, Dr. Konrad. The application on behalf of the Defendant Goering with reference to two witnesses is granted in the sense that the witnesses are to be alerted. The application on behalf of the Defendants Hess and Frank for official information from the ministry of war of the United States of America is denied. That is all.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: I would Like to put another question to you on the subject of the Commando Order. Did the Naval Operations Staff have any part in introducing this order?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: No, no part at ale

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Did you, did the Naval Operations Staff have the possibility, either before or during the drafting of the order, of investigating the correctness of the particulars mentioned in Paragraph 1 of the order?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: No, such a possibility did not exist.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: The treatment of a man who had attacked the Tirpitz with a two-man torpedo in October 1942 has just been discussed here. Did you know that a year later, in the autumn of 1943, there was a renewed attack on the Tirpitz with two-man torpedoes, and that the British sailors who were captured at that time were treated in accordance with the Geneva Convention by the Navy, who had captured them?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: The second attack on the Tirpitz is known to me. I do not remember the treatment of the prisoners.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: You mentioned that the Naval Operations Staff possibly received reports on the statements made by men of Commando units. From what aspect did those reports interest the Naval Operations Staff? Did operational questions interest you, or the personal fate of these people?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: Naturally we were interested in the tactical and operational problems so that we could gather experiences and draw our conclusions from them.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Can you actually remember seeing such a report?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Just now a document was shown to you dealing with the treatment of a Commando unit captured in a Norwegian fiord. It is Number 526-PS. Do you still have that document?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: Possibly, some documents are still lying here.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Will you have a look at that document. I am having the document handed to you. In the third paragraph you will find a reference to the fact that this Commando unit was carrying 1,000 kilograms of explosives. Is that correct?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZB0HLER
turns.text: Did you understand my question?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: I answered "yes."

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: I am sorry, I did not hear you. In the fifth paragraph you will find that the Commando unit had orders to carry out sabotage against strong joints, battery positions, troop barracks, and bridges, and to organize a system for the purpose of further sabotage. Is that correct?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Did these assignments have anything to do with the Navy?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Can you see any indication in the whole document which would suggest that the Navy had anything at all to do with the capture or the treatment of this Commando unit?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: No, the document does not contain an indication of that sort.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: You were asked this morning about the case of the Monte Corbea. In connection with a court-martial ruling against the commander, the Commander-in-Chief of the Navy, Admiral Raeder, sent a wireless message at that time to all commanders. This radiogram is recorded in Document Doenitz-78 in the. document book, Volume 4, Page 230. I shall read that wireless message to you: "The Commander-in-Chief of the Navy has personally and expressly renewed his instructions that all U-boat commanders must adhere strictly to the orders regarding the treatment of neutral ships. Any infringement of these orders has 14 May, 46 incalculable political consequences. This order is to be communicated to all commanders immediately." Do you see any suggestion here that the order is restricted to Spanish ships?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: No, there is no such suggestion in this order.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: I submit to you a document which was used yesterday, D-807. It deals with notes to the Norwegian Government on the sinking of several steamers and contains the drafts of these notes of the High Command of the Navy. Does this document yield any indication at all that the notes were actually sent, or is it impossible to tell from the drafts that the notes themselves were ever dispatched?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: Since there are no initials or signatures on either of these letters, they may be drafts. At any rate, proof that they were actually sent is not apparent from this document.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Did you give us the page number of it?

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: It was submitted yesterday, Mr. President. It is not in any document book.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Yes, I see.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: I now read to you the first sentence from another document which was put to you yesterday. Its number is D-846 and it concerns a discussion with the German Minister to Denmark, Renthe-Fink, on 26 September 1939. I shall read the first sentence to you: "Sinking of Swedish and Finnish ships by our submarines have caused considerable concern here on account of the Danish food transports to Great Britain." Does this report give any indication that these sinkings took place without warning, or were these ships sunk because contraband was captured on them in the course of a legitimate search?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: The sentence which you have just read does not show how these ships were sunk. As far as I remember the document from yesterday, it does not contain any reference to the way in which these ships were sunk, so that it must be assumed as a matter of course that they were sunk in accordance with the Prize Ordinance.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: You were asked yesterday whether you considered the German note to the neutral countries of 24 November 1939 a fair warning against entering certain waters and you answered the question in the affirmative. Is that right?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: And then you were asked whether had deceived the neutrals, and you answered that question with "no." Did this negative answer apply to the previous question on the warning against sailing in certain waters, or did it refer to all the political measures with regard to neutral states which the German Government took in order to conceal their own political intentions?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: The answer in that context referred to the previous questions which had been asked about warning the neutrals promptly of the measures which we adopted for the war at sea.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: I want to make this point quite clear. Do you have any doubt whatever that the pretense of minefields in the operational zones around the British Coast served not only the purpose of deceiving the enemy defense, but also the political purpose of concealing from the neutrals the weapons which we employed in the war at sea?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: Yes, I expressly confirm this two-fold purpose.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: The two-fold purpose of secrecy?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZB0HLER
turns.text: Do you have any doubt whatever that the German Government denied to neutral governments that certain ships were sunk by U-boats, although they had in fact been sunk by U-boats?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: Yes. Or rather, no. I have no doubt that the denials were formulated in that way, as a generally accepted political measure adopted wherever indicated.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Yesterday you admitted the possibility that Admiral Doenitz, as Commander-in-Chief of U-boats, may have received knowledge from the Naval Operations Staff of the handling of political incidents caused by U-boats. Can you, after careful recollection, name a single instance when he did in fact receive from the SKL information on the political measures adopted?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: No, I do not remember such an instance.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: I have no further questions.

turns.speaker: DR. SIEMERS
turns.text: Admiral, you have explained the basis of the Commando Order, as far as the Naval Operations Staff is concerned, by referring to Hitler's definite assertions that he had in his possession enemy orders saying that prisoners were to be killed. In connection with this Commando Order Colonel Phillimore dealt with the case of the British sailor Evans in great detail. In my opinion that case has not so far been clarified. Colonel Phillimore spoke of the murder of a soldier. I think that in spite of the soundness of the documents the Prosecution is mistaken about the facts, also in a legal respect. Will you once more look at both documents, Document D-864... Mr. President, that is Exhibit GB-457, discussed by Colonel Phillimore this morning. This is an affidavit by Gerhard Flesch. The Prosecution quoted the sentence which states that the Commanding Admiral of the Northern Coast of Norway had interrogated Evans personally. Admiral Wagner, does that sentence show that Evans was a prisoner of the Navy?.

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. SIEMERS
turns.text: What was the situation according to the Flesch affidavit? Will you please clarify it?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: According to the second paragraph of that affidavit, Evans must have been in the hands of the SD.

turns.speaker: DR. SIEMERS
turns.text: That is right. And, Mr. President, may I add that at the beginning of the affidavit Flesch states that he was the commander of the Security Police. The Security Police had captured Evans; he was therefore a prisoner of the SD. [Turning to the witness.] Is it correct, therefore, that the British sailor Evans was available to the German admiral in Norway for the sole purpose of being interrogated?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: Undoubtedly.

turns.speaker: DR. SIEMERS
turns.text: And the admiral was interested in interrogating him merely to obtain purely factual information on the attack on the Tirpitz. Is that correct?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: Quite correct.

turns.speaker: DR. SIEMERS
turns.text: May I ask you to look at the next paragraph of the Affidavit D-864? There it mentions Evans' clothes, and says: "It is not known to me that Evans wore a uniform. As far as I can remember, he was wearing blue overalls." Does this mean that Evans was not recognizable as a soldier?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: No, probably not.

turns.speaker: DR. SIEMERS
turns.text: Will you now pass on to the Document UK-57 submitted by Colonel Phillimore? Mr. President, this is Exhibit GB-164 and should be in the original Document Book Keitel, but I think it was newly submitted today. [Turning to the witness.] You have a photostat copy, have you not?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SIEMERS
turns.text: Will you, please, turn to the fourth page. First, a question: Is it possible that this document was known to the Naval Operations Staff? Does the document indicate that it was sent to the Naval Operations Staff?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: These are informal conference notes of the OKW which were apparently not sent to the Naval Operations Staff.

turns.speaker: DR. SIEMERS
turns.text: If I understand it correctly then, this is a document of the Intelligence Service of the OKW, is it not?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: Yes. That is correct.

turns.speaker: DR. SIEMERS
turns.text: Under Figure 2 it says "attempted attack on the battleship Tirpitz." The first part was read by Colonel Phillimore: "Three Englishmen and two Norwegians were held up at the Swedish frontier." Can one, on the strength of this, say that they were presumably apprehended by the Police and not by the Wehrmacht?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: Presumably, yes. Certainly not by the Navy; but probably by the Police, who controlled the frontiers, so far as I know.

turns.speaker: DR. SIEMERS
turns.text: Do you not think, Admiral, that this is not only probable but certain if you think back to the affidavit of 14 November 1945 by Flesch, the commander of the Security Police, who brought Evans from the frontier to Oslo?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: If you take the two together, then in my opinion it is certain; I do not think there is any doubt about it.

turns.speaker: DR. SIEMERS
turns.text: Will you then look at the following sentence? Mr. President, that is under Figure 2, the last sentence of the first paragraph. I quote: "It was possible to take only the civilian-clothed British sailor Robert Paul Evans"-born on such and such a date-"into arrest. The others escaped into Sweden." Therefore, I think we may assume with certainty that Evans was not recognizable as a soldier.

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: Yes, no doubt.

turns.speaker: DR. SIEMERS
turns.text: Then, will you look at the following sentence. There it says-I quote: "Evans had a pistol holster used for carrying weapons under the arm-pit, and he had a knuckle duster."

turns.speaker: SIR DAVID MAXWELL-FYFE
turns.text: My Lord, it says nothing about civilian clothes in the English copy. I do not want to make a bad point, but it is not in my copy.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I am afraid I do not have the document before me. SIR DAVID MAXW1;LL-FYFE: My Lord, the English copy that I have simply has, "However, only the British seaman, Robert Paul Evans, born 14 January 1922, at London, could be arrested. The others escaped into Sweden." My Lord, I think it can be checked afterwards.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Exact reference to the document?

turns.speaker: SIR DAVID MAXWELL-FYFE
turns.text: My Lord, that was Document UK-57, and it is a report of the OKW, Office for Ausland Abwehr, of 4 January 1944.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Did Colonel Phillimore put it in this morning?

turns.speaker: SIR DAVID MAXWELL-FYFE
turns.text: I put it in, My Lord, I think it was-certainly in cross-examining the Defendant Keitel. It has been in before, My Lord.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I see, it has already been put in with this lot.

turns.speaker: DR. SIEMERS
turns.text: I should be grateful to the Tribunal if the mistake were rectified in the English translation. In the German original text the photostat copy is included, therefore the wording "civilian-clothed" must be correct. Witness, we were discussing the sentence-I quote: "Evans had a pistol holster used for carrying weapons under the arm-pit, and he had a knuckle duster." How does this bear on the fact that he was wearing civilian clothes?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: It shows that he...

turns.speaker: DR. SIEMERS
turns.text: Sir David would like me to read the next sentence too: "Acts of force contrary to international law could not be proved against him. Evans made detailed statements regarding the action and, on 19 January 1943, in accordance with the Fuehrer Order, he was shot." How does this bear on the fact that he was wearing civilian clothes? Does this show that he did not act as a soldier in enemy territory should act?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Just a moment. The Tribunal considers that that is a question of law which the Tribunal has got to decide, and not a question for the witness.

turns.speaker: DR. SIEMERS
turns.text: Then I shall forego the answer. May I ask you to turn to the next page of the document and to come back to the Bordeaux case, a similar case which has already been discussed. You have already explained the Bordeaux case insofar as you said that the Naval Operations Staff was not informed about it. I now draw your attention to the sentence at the bottom of Page 3: "After carrying out the explosions, they sank the boats and tried, with the help of the French civilian population, to escape into Spain." Thus did the men concerned in this operation also not act like soldiers?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: That, according to this document, is perfectly clear.

turns.speaker: DR. SIEMERS
turns.text: Thank you. And now one last question. At the end of his examination Colonel Phillimore asked you whether you considered Grossadmiral Raeder and Grossadmiral Doenitz guilty in the cases which have just been discussed, guilty of these murders as he termed them? Now that I have further clarified these cases I should like you to answer the question again.

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: I consider that both admirals are not guilty in these two cases.

turns.speaker: DR. SIEMERS
turns.text: I have no further questions.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Admiral, during cross-examination you explained your views on the Commando Order. I wanted to ask you: Were your views possibly based on the assumption that the order was examined by a superior authority as to its justification before international law?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: Yes. I assumed that the justification for the order was examined by my superiors.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Furthermore, during cross-examination you stated your conception of what happened when a man was handed over to the SD. I wanted to ask you: Did you have this conception already at that time, or has it taken form now that a great deal of material has become known to you?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: There is no question that this conception was considerably influenced by knowledge of a great deal of material.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: You did not, therefore, at that time have the definite conception that the handing over of a man to the SD meant certain death?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: No, I did not have that conception.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Now, a few questions regarding the equipment of the Commando units. Do you not know that automatic arms were found on some members of these units and that, in particular, pistols were carried in such a manner that if, in the event of capture, the man raised his arms, that movement would automatically cause a shot to be fired which would hit the person standing opposite the man with raised arms? Do you know anything about that?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: I have heard of it.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Did you not see pictures of it?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: At the moment I cannot remember seeing such pictures.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Did the Germans also undertake sabotage operations in enemy countries?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: What has it got to do with that, Dr. Laternser?

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: I wanted to ascertain by means of this question whether the witness had knowledge of German sabotage operations, and furthermore, whether he had received reports about the treatment of such sabotage units.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: That is the very thing which we have already ruled cannot be put. You are not suggesting that these actions were taken by way of reprisal for the way in which German sabotage units were treated? We are not trying whether any other powers have committed breaches of international law, or crimes against humanity, or war crimes; we are trying whether these defendants have. The Tribunal has ruled that such questions cannot be put.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Mr. President, I do not know what answer the witness is going to give. I merely wanted, in case, which I do not know...

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: We wanted to know why you were putting the question. You said you were putting the question in order to ascertain whether German sabotage units had been treated in a way which was contrary to international law, or words to that effect, and that is a matter which is irrelevant.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: But, Mr. President, it would show at least that doubt existed about the interpretation of international law with regard to such operations and that would be of importance for the application of the law.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The Tribunal rules that the question is inadmissible.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Witness, you also stated during your crossexamination that until 1944 you were chief of the Operational Department of the Naval Operations Staff. Can you give information on whether there were strong German naval forces or naval transport ships in the Black Sea?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: The strength of naval forces and transport ships in the Black Sea was very slight.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: For what were they mostly needed?

turns.speaker: WAGNER
turns.text: For our own replacements and their protection.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Laternser, how does this arise out of the cross-examination? You are re-examining now, and you are only entitled to ask questions which arise out of the cross-examination. There have been no questions put with reference to the Black Sea.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Mr. President, I learned during the examination that for a long time the witness was chief of the Operational Department; and I concluded that he was one of the few witnesses who could give me information regarding the facts of a very serious accusation raised by the Russian Prosecution, namely, the accusation that 144,000 people had been loaded on to German ships, that at Sebastopol those ships had gone to sea and had then been blown up, and that the prisoners of war on the ships were drowned. The witness could clarify this matter to some extent.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Laternser, you knew, directly this witness began his evidence, what his position was; and you, therefore, could have cross-examined him yourself at the proper time. You are now re-examining; you are only entitled-because we cannot have the time of the Court wasted-you are only entitled to ask him questions which arise out of the cross-examination. In the opinion of the Tribunal this question does not arise out of the cross-examination.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Mr. President, please, would you, as an exception, admit this question? low PRESIDENT: No, Dr. Laternser, the Tribunal has given you a great latitude and we cannot continue to do so. The Tribunal will now adjourn. [The Tribunal recessed until 1400 hours.] Afternoon Session

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: You have finished, have you not, Dr. Kranzbuehler, with this witness?

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The witness can retire. [The witness left the stand.]

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: And now I should like to call my next witness, Admiral Godt. The witness Godt took the stand.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you state your full name?

turns.speaker: EBERHARD GODT (Witness)
turns.text: My name is Eberhard Godt.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you repeat this oath after me: I swear by God-the Almighty and Omniscient-that I will speak the pure truth-and will withhold and add nothing. [The witness repeated the oath.] You may sit down.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Admiral Goat, when did you enter the Navy as an officer cadet?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: On 1 July 1918.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: How long have you been working with Admiral Doenitz, and in what position?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Since January 1938; first of all as First Naval Staff Officer attached to the Commander, U-boats, and immediately after the beginning of the war as Chief of the Operations Department.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Chief of the Operations Department with the Chief of Submarines?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes, attached to the Chief of Submarines, later Flag Officer, U-boats.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZB0HLER
turns.text: Did you collaborate since 1938 in the drafting of all operational orders worked out by the staff of the Flag Officer, U-boats?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: How many officers were on this staff at the beginning of the war?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: At the beginning of the war there were four officers, one chief engineer, and two administrative officers on that staff.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: I shall now show you Document GB-83 of the Prosecution's document book Page 16, which is a letter from Commander, U-boats, dated 9 October 1939. It refers to bases in Norway. How did this letter originate?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: At that time I was visiting the Naval Operations Staff in Berlin on other business. On the occasion of that visit I was asked whether Commander, U-boats, was interested in bases in Norway and what demands should be made in that connection.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Were you informed how those bases in Norway were to be secured for the use of the German Navy?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZB0HLER
turns.text: The Prosecution has quoted an extract from the War Diary of the Naval Operations Staff dating from the same period. Mr. President, I am thinking of the extract reproduced on Page 15 of the document book. [Turning to the witness.] That extract contains four questions. Questions (a) and (d) deal with technical details regarding bases in Norway, whereas (b) and (c) deal with the possibility of obtaining such bases against the will of the Norwegians, and the question of defending them. Which of these questions was put to you?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: May I ask you to repeat the questions in detail first of an.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: The first question is: Which places in Norway can be considered for bases?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: That question was put.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Will you show me from the letter from Commander, U-boats, whether the question was answered and where it is answered? .

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: The question was answered under Number 1 (c) at the end of Number 1.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: There it says, "Trondheim or Narvik are possible places."

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes, that is right.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Question Number 2 is: "If it is impossible to obtain bases without fighting, can it be done against the will of the Norwegians by the use of military force?" Was that question put?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Can you tell me if the question was answered in the letter from Commander, U-boats?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: This question was not answered.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: The third question is: "What are the possibilities of defense after occupation?" Was that question put to you?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No, that question was not put. FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBt1HLER: Is it replied to in the letter?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: III-d refers to the necessity of adopting defense measures.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Is that reference connected with the fourth question I put to you now: "Will the harbors have to be developed to the fullest extent as bases, or do they already offer decisive advantages as possible supply points?"

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: These two questions are not connected.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Was that fourth question put to you?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Was it answered?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Not in this letter.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: What is the significance of the figures II and III? Do they not answer the question of whether these ports must be developed as bases or whether they can be used just as supply points?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: They indicate what was thought necessary in order to develop them to the fullest extent as bases.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Will you please read the last sentence of the document? There it says, "Establishment of a fuel supply point in Narvik as an alternative supply point." Is that not a reply to the question asking whether a supply point is enough?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes; I had overlooked that sentence.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Can I sum up, therefore, by saying that the first and fourth questions were put to you and answered by you, whereas questions 2 and 3 were not put to you and not answered by you?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: In the War Diary of the Naval Operations Staff there is a note which says, "Commander, U-boats, considers such ports extremely valuable even as temporary supply and equipment bases for Atlantic U-boats." Does that note mean that Admiral Doenitz was working on this question before your visit to Berlin? Or what was the reason for the note?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: That was my own opinion, which I was entitled to give in my capacity as Chief of the Operations Department.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Was that the first time that plans for bases were brought to your notice?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No. We had been considering the question of whether the supply position for U-boats could be improved by using ships- in Iceland, for instance.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZB0HLER
turns.text: Were these considerations in any way connected with the question whether one ought to start a war against the country concerned?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZB0HLER
turns.text: I shall now show you Document GB-91. This appears on Page 18 of the Prosecution's document book. It is an operational order issued by Commander, U-boats, on 30 March 1940 and dealing with the Norwegian enterprise. Is it true, that this is your operational order?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: How many days before the beginning of the Norwegian action was that order released?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Approximately ten days.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZB0HLER
turns.text: Paragraph II, Section 5, contains the following sentence: "While entering the harbor and until the troops have been landed, the naval forces will probably fly the British naval ensign, except in Narvik." Is that an order given by Commander, U-boats, to the submarines under his command?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No. That passage appears under the heading: "Information on our own combat forces."

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZB0HLER
turns.text: And what is the meaning of this allusion?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: It means that U-boats were informed that in certain circumstances our own naval units might fly other flags.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZB0HLER
turns.text: Why was that necessary?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: It was necessary so as to prevent possible mistakes in identity.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Are there any other references to mistakes in identity in this order?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Where?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: In Paragraph IV, Section 5.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Will you please read it?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: There it says, "Beware of confusing our own units with enemy forces." FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBt3HLER: Only that sentence. Did this order instruct U-boats to attack Norwegian forces?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Will you please indicate what the order says about that?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: IV, as states, "Only enemy naval forces and troop transports are to be attacked."

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: What was meant by "enemy" forces?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: "Enemy" forces were British, French, and Russian-no, not Russian. It goes on: "No action is to be taken against Norwegian and Danish forces unless they attack our own forces." FLOi7li;NRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER: Will you please look at Paragraph VI-c?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Paragraph VI says: "Steamers may only be attacked when they have been identified beyond doubt as enemy steamers and as troop transports."

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Was Commander, U-boats, informed of the political action taken with regard to incidents caused by submarines?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: In what way?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: U-boats had orders to report immediately by wireless in the case of incidents, and to supplement the report later.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: I do not think you quite understood my question. I asked you, was Commander, U-boats, informed as to how an incident caused by a submarine would later on be settled with a neutral government?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No, not as a rule.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Can you remember any individual case where he was informed?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: I remember the case of the Spanish steamer Montse Corbea. Later on I learned that Spain had been promised reparations. I cannot remember now whether I received the information through official channels or whether I just heard it accidentally.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: I should now like to establish the dates of certain orders which I have already presented to the Tribunal. I shall show you Standing Order Number 171, which is on Page 159 of Volume III of the document book. What is the date on which that order was issued?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: I shall have to look at it first.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Please do.

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: That order must have originated in the winter of 1939-1940. Probably 1939.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: On what do you base that conclusion?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: I base it on the reference made in 4a to equipment for depth charges. This was taken for granted at a later stage. I also gather it from the reference made in 5b to the shifting of masts and colored lights, something which was formulated then for the first time.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Can you tell us the exact month in 1939?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: I assume that it was November.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: I am now going to show you another order, Standing War Order Number 122. It appears on Page 226 in Volume IV of my document book. Up to now all we know is that this order was issued before May 1940. Can-you give us a more exact date?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: This order must have been issued about the same time as the first, that is to say, about November 1939.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Thank you. How was the conduct of U-boat warfare by Commander, U-boats, organized in practice? Will you explain that to us?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: All orders based on questions of international law, et cetera, originated with the Naval Operations Staff. The Naval Operations Staff also reserved for itself the right to determine the locality of the center of operations-for instance, the distribution of U-boats in the Atlantic Theater, the Mediterranean Theater, and the North Sea Theater. Within these various areas U-boat operations were, generally speaking, entirely at the discretion of Commander, U-boats.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Were the standing orders for U-boats given verbally or in writing?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: In writing.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Were there not verbal orders as well?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Verbal instructions personally issued by Commander, U-boats, played a special part and amounted to personal influence on commanders, as well as to explanations of the contents of written orders.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: On what occasions was · that personal influence exerted?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Particularly when reports were being made by the commanders after each action. There must have been very few commanders who did not make a personal and detailed report to Commander, U-boats, after an action.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Was it possible for written orders to be changed in the course of verbal transmission, or even twisted to mean the opposite?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Such a possibility might have existed, but it never actually happened.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: When they made these verbal reports, could the commanders risk expressing opinions which were not those of Commander, U-boats?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Absolutely. Commander, U-boats, even asked his commanders in so many words to give him their personal opinions in every case, so that he could maintain direct personal contact with them and thus remain in close touch with events on the front, so that he could put matters right, where necessary.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Was this personal contact used for the verbal transmission of shady orders?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: The Prosecution holds that an order-apparently a verbal order-existed, prohibiting the entry in the log of measures considered dubious or unjustifiable from the point of view of international law. Did such a general order exist?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No; there was no general order. In certain individual cases-can remember two-an order was given to omit certain matters from the log.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Which cases do you remember?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: The first was the case of the Athenia; and the second was the sinking of a German boat, which was coming from Japan through the blockade, by a German submarine.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Before I ask you to give me details of that, I should like to know the reason for omitting such matters from the log.

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: It was done for reasons of secrecy. U-boat logs were seen by a great many people: First, in the training stations of the U-boat service itself; and, secondly, in numerous offices of the High Command. Special attention had therefore to be paid to secrecy.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: How many copies of each U-boat war log were made?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: I should say six to eight copies.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Did the omission of such an item from the log mean that all documentary evidence was destroyed in every of lice; or did certain of flees keep these documents?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: These records were received by Commander, U-boats, and probably by the Naval Operations Staff as well.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Was there a standing war order prescribing treatment of incidents?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZB0HLER
turns.text: What were the contents?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: It stated that incidents must be reported immediately by wireless and that a supplementary report must be made later, either in writing or by word of mouth.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Does this standing order contain any allusion to the omission of such incidents from the log?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Will you please tell me now how this alteration was made in the log in the case of the Athenia?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: In the case of the Athenia Oberleutnant Lemp reported on returning that he had torpedoed this ship, assuming it to be an auxiliary cruiser. I cannot now tell you exactly whether this was the first time I realized that such a possibility existed or whether the idea that this might possibly have been torpedoed by a German submarine had already been taken into consideration. Lemp was sent to Berlin to make a report and absolute secrecy was ordered with regard to the case.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: By whom?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: By the Naval Operations Staff, after a temporary order had been issued in our department. I ordered the fact to be erased from the war log of the U-boat.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: And that, of course, was on the orders of Admiral Doenitz?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes, or I ordered it on his instructions.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Did you participate in the further handling of this incident?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Only with regard to the question of whether Lemp should be punished. As far as I remember, Commander, U-boats, took only disciplinary action against him because it was in his favor that the incident occurred during the first few hours of the war, and it was held that in his excitement he had not investigated the character of the ship as carefully as he might have done.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Did I understand you correctly as saying that the detailed documentary evidence in connection with the sinking of the Athenia was retained by both Commander, U-boats, and, you believe, the Naval Operations Staff?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: I can say that with certainty only as far as Commander, U-boats, is concerned. That is what happened in this case.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: You mentioned a second case just now where a log book had been altered. Which case was that?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: That incident was as follows: A German blockade breaker, that is to say, a merchant vessel on its way back from Japan, was accidentally torpedoed by a German submarine and sunk in the North Atlantic. This fact was omitted from the log.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: So it was only a question of keeping matters secret from German offices?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes. The British learned the facts from lifeboats as far as I know; and these facts were to be concealed from the crews of other blockade-breaking vessels.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Documents submitted to the Tribunal by the Defense show that until the autumn of 1942, German U-boats took steps to rescue crews as far as was possible without prejudicing the U-boat's safety and without interfering with their own assignment. Does this agree with your own experiences?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: I should now like to put a few questions to you regarding the so-called Laconia order which still require clarification. I refer to Document GB-199. As you know, the Prosecution calls this order an order to kill survivors. Who formulated this order?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Where is it?

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: It is the document book of the Prosecution on Page 36, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: I cannot now tell you that for certain. Generally speaking, such an order was discussed by Commander, U-boats, the First Naval Staff Officer, and myself; Commander, U-boats, decided on the general terms of the order and then it was formulated by one of us. It is quite possible that I myself worded the order.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: But, at any rate, Admiral Doenitz signed it, did he not?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: He must have; yes.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Admiral Doenitz thought that he remembered that you and Captain Hessler were opposed to this order. Can you remember this, too; and if so, why were you against it?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: I do not remember that.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: What was the meaning of the order?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: The meaning of the order is plain. It prohibited attempts at rescue.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Why was that not forbidden by a reference to Standing War Order Number 154, which was issued in the winter of 1939-40?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Kranzbuehler, surely a written order must speak for itself. Unless there is some colloquial meaning in a particular word used in the order, the order must be interpreted according to the ordinary meaning of the words.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: I was not proposing to go into the question any further, Mr. President. [Turning to the witness.]   I should like to repeat my last question. Why, instead of issuing a new order, did they not simply refer commanders to Standing War Order Number 154, which was issued in the winter of 1939-40? I refer, Mr. President, to Document GB-196, on Page 33 of the Prosecution's document book. You remember that order, don't you. I have shown it to you.

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes, I do. That order had already been canceled when the so-called Laconia order was issued. Apart from that, a mere reference to an order already issued would have lacked the character of actuality which orders should have.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Do you mean by that that your staff, as a matter of principle, did not issue orders by references to earlier orders?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: That was avoided whenever possible; that is to say, almost always.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Will you explain to me why that order was issued as "top secret"?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: The order appeared after an operation An which we nearly lost two boats, and contained a severe reprimand for the commanders concerned. It was not customary for us to put such a reprimand in a form accessible to any one except the commanders and all the officers.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Which is the severe reprimand?

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Will you please explain of what this severe reprimand for the commander consisted?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: It is understandable in the light of previous events- namely, those very things which it forbids. It is largely contained in the sentence beginning: "Rescue is against the most elementary demands" and it is also implied by the harshness, whereby the commander is reproached with being softhearted.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Does this mean that the commanders were accused of having endangered their boats too much in connection with the rescue action of the Laconia and of acting in a manner which was not in accordance with the dictates of war?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes, and that after having been repeatedly reminded during the action of the necessity for acting in a manner in accordance with the dictates of war.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZB0HLER
turns.text: You were interrogated on this order after the capitulation, as you told me; but you could not at the moment remember its exact wording. How was it possible for you not to remember this order?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: There were certain orders which had to be kept in collective files and which one therefore saw very frequently. This order was not one of them, but was filed separately after being dealt with. After it had been issued I never saw it again until the end of the war.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: What did an order intended for inclusion in such a collection look like on the outside?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: It had to be a "Current Order" or an "Admonition Message."

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Did that occur in the text of the order concerned?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: It would be in the heading of the order concerned. That is not the case here.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: So we may conclude from the fact that this wireless message is not headed either "Admonition Message" or "Current Order" that it did not belong to a collection of orders?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: But then how is it possible that Korvettenkapitan Mohle gave lectures on this order apparently until the end of the war?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Korvettenkapitan Mohle had access to all wireless messages issued by Commander, U-boats. He was entitled to select from these signals anything he thought necessary for the instruction of commanders about to go to sea. It made no difference whether the order was marked "Admonition" or "Current Order." He had obviously taken out this message and had had it among the material to be used for these instructions to the commanders.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Did Mohle ever ask you about the interpretation of that order?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZB0HLER
turns.text: Did you ever hear of any other source interpreting this order to mean that survivors were to be shot?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Can you judge from your own experience whether this order had, or could have had, any effect practically on Allied naval losses?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: That is very difficult to judge. At that time something like 80 per cent of all U-boat attacks were probably carried out under conditions which made any attempt at rescue impossible. That is to say, these attacks were made on convoys or on vessels in close proximity to the coast. The fact that some 12 captains and engineers were brought back as prisoners by U-boats is an indication of what happened in the other cases. It is difficult to say with any degree of certainty whether it was possible to take rescue measures in all cases. The situation was probably such that the Allied sailors felt safer in the lifeboats than they did, for instance, aboard the U-boat and probably were glad to see the U-boat vanish after the attack. The fact that the presence of the U-boat involved danger to itself is proved by this same case of the Laconia, where two U-boats were attacked from the air while engaged in rescuing the survivors. I do not think it is at all certain that this order had any effect one way or the other.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: What do you mean "one way or the other"?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: I mean whether it meant an increase or a decrease in the number of losses among enemy seamen.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: There is one argument I did not quite understand. You pointed to the fact that approximately 12 captains and chief engineers were made prisoner after this order was issued. Do you mean by that that only in these few cases was it possible, without endangering the submarine, to carry out the order to transfer such officers from the lifeboats?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: It is too much to say that it was only possible in these few cases, but it does afford some indication of the number of cases in which it was possible.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: I shall now show you the wireless message which went to Kapitanleutnant Schacht. It is on Page 36 of the Prosecution's document book. This message, too, was sent as "top secret." What was the reason for that?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: It is a definite and severe reprimand for the commander.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: How far was that reprimand justified? Schacht had not received previous instruction to rescue Italians only?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No, but it had been assumed that U-boats would realize that it was of primary importance that allies should be rescued, that is, that they should not become prisoners of war. Apart from that, several reminders had been issued in the course of operations warning commanders to be particularly careful. After that came Schacht's report, which appeared at the time to indicate that he had disobeyed orders. Viewed retrospectively, Schacht's action must have taken place before Commander, U-boats, issued the order in question, so that in part at least, the accusation was unjustified.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Were any further rescue measures carried out by U-boats after this order was issued in September 1942?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: In isolated cases, yes.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Did Commander, U-boats, object to these rescues?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: I have no recollection of that.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: To your knowledge, did German U-boats deliberately kill survivors?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: The only case I know of-and I heard of it after the capitulation-is that of Kapitanleutnant Eck. We heard an enemy broadcast which hinted at these happenings, but we were unable to draw any conclusions from that.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: I now hand to you the Prosecution's Exhibit GB-203, which is regarded by the Prosecution as proof of the shooting of survivors. This is the war log of U-247 from which I mimeographed an extract on Page 74 of Volume II of my document book. This extract describes an attack made by the U-boat on a British trawler. You have already seen this war log. After his return, did the commander make a report on this action?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Did he report anything about the shooting of survivors on that occasion?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: According to a statement made by a survivor named McAllister this trawler, the Noreen Mary, had a gun aboard. Do you know whether trawlers had guns mounted fore or aft?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: They were almost always in the bows.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Can you remember, with the help of this extract from the war log and on the strength of your own recollection of the commander's report, the exact details of this incident?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Originally the U-boat when submerged encountered a number of vessels escorting trawlers close to Cape Wrath. It tried to torpedo one of the trawlers.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Is the witness trying to reconstruct this from the document, reconstruct the incident?

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: I am asking him to tell us what he remembers of the event, basing his account on his own recollection of the commander's report supplemented by the entry in the war log.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Well, he hasn't said whether he ever saw the commander.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Oh yes, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Well then, all he can tell us is what the commander told him.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Well, have him do that then.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Will you please tell us what you remember after reading the log.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Wait a minute. If he remembers anything about what the commander told him he can tell us that, but the log speaks for itself and he can't reconstruct it out of that. He must tell us what he remembers of what the officer said.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Very well, Sir. [Turning to the witness.] Will you please speak from memory.

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: The commander reported that he had encountered a number of trawlers extraordinarily close to the coast, considering conditions at the time. Failing in his attempt to torpedo one of them, he sank it with gunfire. That was all the more remarkable because, in the first place, the incident occurred quite unusually near the coast and, in the second place, the commander risked this artillery fight regardless of the presence of other vessels nearby.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Were these other ships also armed trawlers?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: It was to be assumed at the time that every trawler was armed.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: The witness McAllister thought that the submarine surfaced 50 yards away from the trawler. In the light of your own recollections and experiences, do you think this is possible?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: I do not remember the details; but it would be an unusual thing for a U-boat commander to do.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZB0HLER
turns.text: McAllister also stated the U-boat used shells filled with wire.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Wait a minute. Wait a minute. Dr. Kranzbuehler, the Tribunal thinks that the witness oughtn't to express opinions of this sort. He ought to give us the evidence of any facts which he has. He is telling us in his opinion it is impossible that a naval commander would ever bring his submarine up within 50 yards of another vessel. FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBt1HLER: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: That is not a matter for him to say.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Mr. President, I was going to ask the witness next whether German U-boats used shells filled with wire as stated by the witness McAllister. Is that question admissible?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Shells filled with wire?

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Yes, that is the question I want to put. Will you answer that question, Witness.

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: There were no such shells.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Was this attack by the submarine on the Noreen Mary reported by wireless immediately? Do you know anything about it?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Do you mean the U-boat commanders report?

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: No, by the British.

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: As far as I remember, a wireless message sent by a British vessel was intercepted, reporting a U-boat attack in the area.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: A signal is entered in the war log under 0127 hours. It is intended for Matschulat, which means that it was sent by you to the commander, and it reads, "English steamer reports attack by German U-boat west of Cape Wrath."

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: That is the message intended to inform the U-boat that a wireless signal sent by a British steamer concerning a submarine attack in that area had been intercepted.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: I should now like to ask you something about Standing War Order Number 511. This is in Volume I of my document book, Page 46. When I presented this order, the Tribunal was not sure of the significance of Paragraph 2, which I am going to read: "Captains and officers of neutral ships which may be sunk according to Standing Order Number 101, (such as Swedish except Goteborg traffic), must not be taken on board, since internment of these officers is not permitted by international law." Can you tell me first the experiences or calculations which led to the inclusion of Paragraph 2 in the order?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: On one occasion a U-boat brought a Uruguayan officer -a captain whose ship had been sunk-to Germany. We were afraid that if we released this captain he might report some of the things he had seen while he was interned aboard the U-boat. The reason for this order was to avoid difficulties of that kind in the future; for the Uruguayan captain had to be released and was, in fact, released.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: What is the meaning of the reference to neutral ships which might be sunk according to Standing War Order Number 101?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: May I please see the order for a minute? [The document was submitted to the witness.] The Standing War Order Number 101 contains the following directives in connection with the sinking of neutral ships: Once inside the blockade zone, all neutral ships can be sunk as a matter of principle, with two main exceptions, or shall we say, two general exceptions. To begin with, ships belonging to certain neutral countries, with whom agreements had been made regarding definite shipping channels, must not be sunk; further, ships belonging to certain neutral states which might be assumed not to be working exclusively in the enemy's service. Outside the blockade zone neutral ships might be sunk; first, if they were not recognizable as neutrals and therefore must be regarded as enemy vessels by the submarine in question and, second, if they were not acting as neutrals. · °

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: As, for instance, those traveling in enemy convoy?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes, those traveling in convoys, or if they reported the presence of U-boats, et cetera, by wireless.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Did Paragraph 2 mean that the captains of neutral ships would in the future be in a worse position than captains of enemy ships, or would they be in a better position?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: This is not a question of better or worse, it is a question of taking prisoners. They were not to be taken prisoners because they could not be detained as such: Whether this meant that their positions would be better or worse is at least open to doubt. Captains of enemy ships usually tried to avoid being taken aboard the U-boat probably because they felt safer in their lifeboats.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: What do you know about orders to respect hospital ships at the beginning of the invasion?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: At the beginning of the invasion the rule in this area, as in any other area, was that hospital ships were not to be attacked. Commanders operating in the invasion zone then reported that there was a very large number of hospital ships sailing.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: From where to where?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Between the Normandy invasion area and the British Isles. Commander, U-boats, then had investigations made by the competent department as to whether hospital traffic was really as heavy as alleged in these reports. That was found to be the case.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: What do you mean by that?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: It means that the number of hospital ships reported corresponded to the estimated number of wounded. After that it was expressly announced that hospital ships were not to be attacked in the future.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Was the strict respect paid to hospital ships at that stage of the war in our own interests?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: At that time we only had hospital ships in the Baltic where the Geneva Convention was not recognized by the other side; so eve had no particular interest in respecting hospital ships.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Do you know of any case of an enemy hospital ship being sunk by a German U-boat during this war?

turns.speaker: O GODT
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Did it happen the other way round?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: The German hospital ship Tubingen was, I think, sunk by British aircraft in the Mediterranean.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Presumably because of mistaken identity?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Kranzbuehler, the question on German hospital ships which were sunk isn't relevant, is it?

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: I was going to show by it, Mr. President, that the possibility of mistaken identity does exist and that a hospital ship was in fact sunk in consequence of such a mistake. My evidence therefore goes to show that from the sinking of a ship it must not be concluded that the sinking was ordered.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The Tribunal quite realize that mistakes may be made in sea warfare. It is a matter of common knowledge. Should re-adjourn now?

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Yes, Mr. President. [A recess was taken.]

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Admiral Godt, you have known Admiral Doenitz very well since 1934; and you have had a good deal to do with him during that time. Did he have anything to do with politics during that time?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Nothing at all, to my knowledge, before he was appointed Commander-in-Chief of the Navy. As Commander-in-Chief of the Navy he made occasional speeches outside the Navy; for instance, he addressed dock workers, made a speech to the Hitler Youth at Stettin, and gave a talk over the air on "Heroes' Day" and on 20 July; I remember no other occasions.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Were these speeches not always directly connected with the tasks of the Navy-for instance, the address to the dock laborers-ship-building?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes, when he spoke to the dock laborers.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: And to the Hitler Youth?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: The Hitler Youth, too.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: And what was the connection there?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: As far as I remember, the speech was concerned with recruiting for the Navy.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Did he select his staff officers for their ideological or military qualifications?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Their military and personal qualities were all that mattered. Their political views had nothing to do with it.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: The question of whether Admiral Doenitz knew, or must have known, of certain happenings outside the Navy is a very important one from the Tribunal's point of view. Can you tell me who his associates were?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: His own officers and officers of his own ages almost exclusively. As far as I know, he had very few contacts beyond those.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Did matters change much in this respect after he was appointed Commander-in-Chief of the Navy?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No. He probably had a few more contacts with people from other branches, but on the whole his circle remained the same.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Where did he actually live at that time, that is, after his appointment as Commander-in-Chief of the Navy?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: After his appointment as Commander-in-Chief, he was mainly at the headquarters of the Naval Operations Staff near Berlin.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Did he live with his family or with his staff?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: He made his home with his family; but the main part of his life was spent with his staff.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: And where did he live when his staff was transferred to the so-called "Koralle" quarters in the neighborhood of Berlin in the autumn of 1943?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: He lived at his headquarters, where his family also lived-at least for some time. His official discussions, however, usually lasted till late in the evening.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: In, other words, from that time on he lived constantly in the naval officers' quarters?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: You were in a better position than almost any of the other officers to observe the Admiral's career at close quarters. Can you tell me what you think were the motives behind the military orders he issued?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: You can't speak about the motives of people. You can't give evidence about other people's minds. You can only give evidence of what they said and what they did.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Mr. President, I still think that an officer who lived with another officer for years must have a certain knowledge of his motives, based on the actions of the officer in question and on what that officer told him. However, perhaps I may put my question rather differently.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: He can give evidence about his character, but he can't give evidence about his motives.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Then I shall question him on his character, Your Honor. Witness, can you tell me whether Admiral Doenitz ever expressed selfish motives to you in connection with any other orders he gave or any of his actions?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Kranzbuehler, that is the same thing, the same question again, really.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: I beg your pardon, Mr. President. I meant it to be a different question.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Nobody is charging him with being egotistical or anything of that sort. He is charged with the various crimes that are charged against him in the Indictment.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Then I shall ask a direct question based on the Prosecution's opinion. The Prosecution judged Admiral Doenitz to be cynical and opportunistic. Does that agree with your own judgment?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: How would you judge him?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: As a man whose mind was fixed entirely on duty, on his work, his naval problems, and the men in his service.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Mr. President, I have no further questions to put to this witness.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Do any other members of the defendants' counsel want to ask any questions? there was no response.]

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: My Lord, might I first mention the documents that I put in in cross-examination this morning, or rather it was a document which had been in before. It was D-658, GB-229. That is the document dealing with Bordeaux, and there was a dispute as to whether it was from the Bordeaux Commando Raid. The dispute was as to whether it was from the SKL, that is the Naval War Staff Diary, or from the war diary of some lower formation. My Lord, I have had the matter confirmed with the Admiralty, and I will produce the original for defense counsel; it comes from the SKL War Diary, Tagebuch der Seekriegsleitung, and it is from Number 1 Abteilung, Tell A-that is part A-for December 1942. So it is from the War Diary of the Defendant Raeder and the witness. You have said, Witness, that you don't recollect protesting against this order of 17 September 1942.

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: I will try and refresh your memory. Would you look at a document, D-865? That's GB-458, My Lord; that is an extract from an interrogation of Admiral Doenitz on 6 October. I should say that the record was kept in English and therefore the translation into German does not represent necessarily the Admiral's actual words. [Turning to the witness.] Would you look at the second page of that document at the end of the first paragraph. It is the end of the first paragraph on Page 207 in the English text. The Admiral is dealing with the order of 17 September 1942, and in that last sentence in that paragraph he says: "I remember that Captain Goat and Captain Hessler were opposed to this telegram. They said so expressly because, as they said, 'it might be misunderstood.' But I said, 'I must pass it on now to these boats to prevent this 1 percent of losses. I must give them a reason, so that they do not feel themselves obliged to do that." Do you remember protesting now, saying "That can be misunderstood"?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No, I do not recall that.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: And a further extract on Page 3 of the English translation, the bottom of Page 2 of the German: "So I sent a second telegram to prevent further losses. The second telegram was sent at my suggestion. I am completely and personally responsible for it, because both Captain Godt and Captain Hessler expressly stated they thought the telegram ambiguous or liable to misinterpretation." Do you remember that now?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No, I do not recall that.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: Would you look at a further statement to the same effect, on Page 5 of the English, first paragraph; Page 4 of the German text, third paragraph. He has been asked the question: "Why was it necessary to use a phrase like the one that I read to you before: Efforts to rescue members of the crew were counter to the most elementary demands of warfare for the destruction of enemy ships and crews?" It is the last clause of the first sentence, and he answered: "These words do not correspond to the telegram. They do not in any way correspond to our actions in the years of 1939, 1940, 1941 and 1942, as I have plainly shown you through the Laconia incident. I would like to emphasize once more that both Captain Godt and Captain Hessler were violently opposed to the dispatch of this telegram." Do you still say that you don't remember protesting against the sending of that telegram? GODT; I have stated repeatedly that I do not remember it.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: I will show you one more extract, Document D-866, which will become GB-459. That is a further interrogation on 22 October. The first question on the document is: "Do you believe that this order is contrary to the Prize Regulations issued by the German Navy at the beginning of the war?" And the last sentence of the first paragraph of the answer is: "Godt and Hessler said to me, 'Don't send this message. You see, it might look odd some day. It might be misinterpreted.' " You don't remember using those words?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: You were an experienced staff officer, were you not?

turns.speaker: GODT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. PHILLIMORE
turns.text: You knew the importance of drafting an GODT: Yes. COL. PHILLIMORE: These orders you were issuing were going to young commanders between 20 and 30 years of age' were they not? GODT: Certainly not as young as 20. They would be in their late twenties, most probably. COL. PHILLIMORE: Yes. Do you say that this order is not ambiguous? GODT: Yes. Perhaps if you take one sentence out of the context you might have some doubt, but not if you read the entire order. 544 14 May 46 COL. PHILLIMORE: What was the point of the words: "Rescue runs counter to the most elementary demands of warfare for the destruction of enemy ships and crews"? [There was no response.] Show it to him, will you? [The document was submitted to the witness.] What was the point of those words, if this was merely a nonrescue order? GODT: It was served to motivate the remainder of the order and to put on an equal level all the ships and crews which were fighting against our U-boats. COL. PHILLIMORE: You see, all your orders were so clear, were they not? Have you got the Defense documents there in the witness box? GODT: I think so-no. COL. PHILLIMORE: Look at the Defense Document Number D5nitz-8, Page 10. It is on Page 10 of that book.
